SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly articulated and are supported by extensive empirical evaluation across tasks, models and varying data sizes, with reasonable baselines reported. However, there is no formal theoretical or mechanistic analysis explaining why information is encoded nonlinearly in some embeddings, and the summary does not indicate rigorous statistical significance testing or confounder analyses for ranking stability — shortcomings I penalize as a theory-oriented reviewer. Experimental methodology appears broadly sound (diverse datasets, models and hyperparameter reporting), and stated limitations show awareness of overfitting and computational issues, so defects are minor rather than fatal to the conclusions. | B(4): The paper makes clear, empirically testable claims and supports them with a broad suite of supervised tasks, multiple model families (linear and nonlinear), varying training-data regimes, and many datasets (e.g., SimLex999, SST, POS, WordNet), with code and hyperparameters released — all strong points for reproducibility. I downgrade one point because the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or formal controls for selection/bias across tasks (rank stability and mean-rank changes are reported but significance bounds are unclear), and the heavy computational cost/overfitting issues for some setups suggest some methodological fragility. Overall the experiments are sound and appropriately target the claims, but a couple of empirical rigor details are missing.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly articulated and are supported by extensive empirical evaluation across tasks, models and varying data sizes, with reasonable baselines reported. However, there is no formal theoretical or mechanistic analysis explaining why information is encoded nonlinearly in some embeddings, and the summary does not indicate rigorous statistical significance testing or confounder analyses for ranking stability — shortcomings I penalize as a theory-oriented reviewer. Experimental methodology appears broadly sound (diverse datasets, models and hyperparameter reporting), and stated limitations show awareness of overfitting and computational issues, so defects are minor rather than fatal to the conclusions.

I downgrade one point because the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or formal controls for selection/bias across tasks (rank stability and mean-rank changes are reported but significance bounds are unclear), and the heavy computational cost/overfitting issues for some setups suggest some methodological fragility. Overall the experiments are sound and appropriately target the claims, but a couple of empirical rigor details are missing.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized with a clear flow from motivation to method and experiments, and the main contributions are stated early (focus on data efficiency and supervised tasks). Notation and experimental setup are presented consistently (datasets, baselines, metrics, and hyperparameters are reported), and figures/tables summarizing performance across data subsets make comparisons and rank stability easy to follow. Writing quality is generally good with adequate related-work coverage, though a few sections could benefit from slightly tighter signposting or more explicit captioning to aid readers less familiar with evaluation conventions. | B(4): The paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early, focusing on data efficiency and supervised probes for embeddings. Notation and experimental details (datasets, baselines, metrics, hyperparameters) are reported consistently, aiding reproducibility—code is available. Figures/tables and examples appear informative for comparing embeddings and model types, though a few methodological caveats (e.g., computational cost of the regression network and overfitting on shared-word splits) could be more explicitly emphasized in the presentation. Overall writing is good with only minor presentation clarifications needed for the most computationally intensive analyses.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear flow from motivation to method and experiments, and the main contributions are stated early (focus on data efficiency and supervised tasks). Notation and experimental setup are presented consistently (datasets, baselines, metrics, and hyperparameters are reported), and figures/tables summarizing performance across data subsets make comparisons and rank stability easy to follow. Writing quality is generally good with adequate related-work coverage, though a few sections could benefit from slightly tighter signposting or more explicit captioning to aid readers less familiar with evaluation conventions.

Notation and experimental details (datasets, baselines, metrics, hyperparameters) are reported consistently, aiding reproducibility—code is available. Figures/tables and examples appear informative for comparing embeddings and model types, though a few methodological caveats (e.g., computational cost of the regression network and overfitting on shared-word splits) could be more explicitly emphasized in the presentation. Overall writing is good with only minor presentation clarifications needed for the most computationally intensive analyses.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experimental design is broad and systematic—multiple supervised tasks, both linear and nonlinear models, and evaluation across varying training-data sizes—so it convincingly supports the main empirical claims about data-efficiency and task-dependent rankings. The paper also reports hyperparameters, shares code, and discusses several failure modes (e.g., overfitting in analogy datasets), which helps reproducibility and transparency. However, from a theory-purist perspective it lacks mechanistic or formal analysis explaining why embeddings encode information nonlinearly, and I see no mention of rigorous statistical significance testing or controls for confounders; these omissions reduce confidence in the generality of some conclusions. Overall, strong empirical breadth but missing the theoretical explanations and statistical rigor I expect. | B(4): The paper provides a solid, empirically-driven evaluation: it varies training data size, compares linear vs nonlinear downstream models, measures rank stability, and covers a wide suite of supervised tasks and datasets (similarity, analogy, sentence, POS, sentiment, etc.), which strongly supports the main claims about data efficiency and model-dependent encoding. Sensitivity analyses are present via learning-curve-style evaluations and ranking-change metrics; common failure modes (overfitting on shared-word splits, computational cost of regression NN) are explicitly discussed. Reproducibility is good—code and hyperparameters are released—so results should be repeatable. There remain minor gaps (e.g., potential additional ablations over embedding families or more efficient alternatives to the costly NN) that prevent an excellent (5) rating.

reviewer_a.justification: The experimental design is broad and systematic—multiple supervised tasks, both linear and nonlinear models, and evaluation across varying training-data sizes—so it convincingly supports the main empirical claims about data-efficiency and task-dependent rankings. The paper also reports hyperparameters, shares code, and discusses several failure modes (e.g., overfitting in analogy datasets), which helps reproducibility and transparency. However, from a theory-purist perspective it lacks mechanistic or formal analysis explaining why embeddings encode information nonlinearly, and I see no mention of rigorous statistical significance testing or controls for confounders; these omissions reduce confidence in the generality of some conclusions. Overall, strong empirical breadth but missing the theoretical explanations and statistical rigor I expect.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides a solid, empirically-driven evaluation: it varies training data size, compares linear vs nonlinear downstream models, measures rank stability, and covers a wide suite of supervised tasks and datasets (similarity, analogy, sentence, POS, sentiment, etc.), which strongly supports the main claims about data efficiency and model-dependent encoding. Sensitivity analyses are present via learning-curve-style evaluations and ranking-change metrics; common failure modes (overfitting on shared-word splits, computational cost of regression NN) are explicitly discussed. Reproducibility is good—code and hyperparameters are released—so results should be repeatable. There remain minor gaps (e.g., potential additional ablations over embedding families or more efficient alternatives to the costly NN) that prevent an excellent (5) rating.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper evaluates a broad set of appropriate baselines (unsupervised cosine measures, classic analogy methods 3COSADD/3COSMUL, and a diverse set of supervised learners including linear and nonlinear models) across many standard datasets (SimLex, MEN, WS353, WordRep, SST, POS, WordNet, etc.). Comparisons appear fair: same datasets, reported hyperparameters, code available, and metrics reported, and the authors explicitly acknowledge limitations and failure modes (e.g., overfitting on shared vocabularies, computational cost of neural analogy). They also analyze how rankings change with model class and data size, indicating careful comparative analysis and discussion of prior methods’ relative performance. | B(5): The paper compares against a comprehensive set of baselines (cosine, 3COSADD/3COSMUL, and a wide range of supervised models including RF, SVR, SVM, CNN, k-NN, Naive Bayes and regression neural nets) and evaluates them on the same datasets and metrics while reporting hyperparameters and releasing code, supporting fair reproducibility. It explicitly acknowledges when prior methods perform comparably or have limitations (e.g., overfitting in analogy datasets, computational cost of the regression NN) and analyzes differences by varying model family and training data size to expose nonlinear encoding and rank instability. Related work coverage is reported as adequate, and the evaluation protocol (data-efficiency curves, linear vs nonlinear splits) provides clear, practical comparisons that are directly useful for model selection.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates a broad set of appropriate baselines (unsupervised cosine measures, classic analogy methods 3COSADD/3COSMUL, and a diverse set of supervised learners including linear and nonlinear models) across many standard datasets (SimLex, MEN, WS353, WordRep, SST, POS, WordNet, etc.). Comparisons appear fair: same datasets, reported hyperparameters, code available, and metrics reported, and the authors explicitly acknowledge limitations and failure modes (e.g., overfitting on shared vocabularies, computational cost of neural analogy). They also analyze how rankings change with model class and data size, indicating careful comparative analysis and discussion of prior methods’ relative performance.

It explicitly acknowledges when prior methods perform comparably or have limitations (e.g., overfitting in analogy datasets, computational cost of the regression NN) and analyzes differences by varying model family and training data size to expose nonlinear encoding and rank instability. Related work coverage is reported as adequate, and the evaluation protocol (data-efficiency curves, linear vs nonlinear splits) provides clear, practical comparisons that are directly useful for model selection.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important, practical evaluation problem for word embeddings and uses rigorous, reproducible empirical methodology making it a good fit for an NLP/ML venue. Reviewer A’s reservations about the lack of theoretical/mechanistic analysis temper enthusiasm but do not negate the paper’s clear relevance and utility; given its strong empirical contribution and target audience, a slightly above-mid appropriateness score (4) reflects this balance.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a central evaluation problem for NLP/ML venues and uses the expected suite of supervised tasks, multiple baselines, and rigorous empirical protocol (data-size sweeps, linear vs nonlinear models, many datasets, hyperparameters reported, code released). However, from the provided summary the contribution is primarily empirical: it lacks principled theoretical or mechanistic explanation for why embeddings encode information nonlinearly or how data-efficiency differences arise, which a top conference audience (and my own theoretical standards) would expect alongside empirical claims. Thus it is borderline appropriate — valuable and relevant but missing the deeper analytical component that would make it a clear excellent fit.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it tackles a core empirical evaluation problem for word embeddings, emphasizes practical criteria (data efficiency, simple supervised tasks), and evaluates across many realistic datasets and model families. The methodology is empirically thorough (varying training data, linear vs nonlinear models, multiple tasks), reports hyperparameters and releases code, which matches my reproducibility and applied-evaluation priorities. Its focus and audience clearly target researchers and practitioners who need robust, actionable embedding selection criteria, and the work is positioned appropriately within the community.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper provides a valuable, practical reframing of embedding evaluation—emphasizing data efficiency, simple supervised tasks, and learning-curve analyses—and the methodology is thorough and reproducible. It is not a novel algorithmic or theoretical breakthrough, but the empirical contribution and clear guidance for practitioners are substantial enough to merit a positive originality score of 4 rather than a middle or low rating.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper advances a useful perspective—prioritizing data-efficiency and simple supervised tasks and comparing linear vs nonlinear downstream models across training set sizes—which is a clear, practical reframing of embedding evaluation rather than a novel algorithmic invention. The methodology is thorough (multiple datasets, varied models, code released) and distinguishes itself from cosine-based benchmarks, but it remains an empirical evaluation framework without a principled theoretical or mechanistic contribution. From my Theory Purist viewpoint this is a solid and informative empirical study that clarifies evaluation choices, yet it is incremental rather than a fundamentally new idea.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper offers a notable and practically useful perspective shift—prioritizing data efficiency and simple supervised downstream tasks (with both linear and nonlinear models and learning-curve analyses) rather than relying solely on traditional unsupervised cosine benchmarks. The methodology (varying training data sizes across a broad suite of tasks and model families) clearly differentiates it from standard intrinsic evaluations and surfaces nontrivial findings (e.g., nonlinear encodings and task-dependent rankings). From my empirical-pragmatist view this is a meaningful, reproducible contribution (code and hyperparameters provided) that would inform practitioners and experts, though it is more of a principled evaluation proposal than an entirely novel modeling technique.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper fills an important practical gap by proposing a reproducible, data‑aware evaluation protocol that will influence embedding evaluation and model selection. The work is primarily empirical and stops short of providing deep theoretical explanations, so it is not transformative in a theory sense, but its clear, actionable guidance and potential to change practice justify a strong positive impact score.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses an important and practical problem—how to evaluate pretrained word embeddings under realistic supervised-data constraints—and its finding that embedding rankings depend on model nonlinearity and data size is useful for practitioners. However, from a theory‑purist perspective the contribution is primarily an empirical evaluation protocol without accompanying mechanistic or formal analysis explaining why embeddings differ (the summary reports empirical comparisons across models and datasets but no theoretical explanation or mention of formal significance testing). The work is likely to change evaluation practice and spur follow-up studies, but it is not yet transformative because it lacks principled, generalizable theory or provable insights that would reshape representation learning more broadly.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses an important practical gap in embedding evaluation by emphasizing data efficiency and simple supervised tasks, and it demonstrates across many datasets (SimLex999, STS, POS, sentiment, WordNet, etc.) that embeddings’ relative quality depends on model class and training-data size. From an empirical-pragmatist perspective the proposed protocol—varying data regimes, comparing linear vs nonlinear downstream models, and releasing code and hyperparameters—provides actionable, reproducible guidance that is likely to change evaluation practice and model selection. While not overturning theory, it strongly nudges researchers and practitioners toward more realistic, task- and data-aware benchmarks and should stimulate follow-up work on robust, data-efficient representations.

main_claims: Evaluation of word embeddings should focus on data efficiency and simple supervised tasks rather than solely unsupervised cosine-based benchmarks.
Including both linear and nonlinear downstream models reveals that information in embeddings can be nonlinearly encoded, producing different rankings depending on model type and data size.

The proposed evaluation uncovers differences between embeddings (e.g., GloVe100 vs GloVe300) and shows there is no universally best embedding; rankings are task dependent and vary with available data.
_summary.methodology: Vary the amount of training data and evaluate pretrained embeddings on a suite of supervised tasks (Similarity, Analogy, Sentence, Single-word) using both linear and nonlinear models, reporting performance across data subsets to measure data efficiency and rank stability.
limitations: Regression neural network approach for analogy is very computationally intensive.
Models easily overfit when train and test sets share the same words (observed for MSR and Google analogy datasets).
Embeddings orderings are very task dependent (no universally good embeddings).

reviewer_a.name: Theory Purist
reviewer_a.background: Senior researcher in computational linguistics and representation learning with a track record of theoretical work on embedding geometry and provable properties.
traits: Penalises papers that present mainly empirical comparisons without accompanying formal or mechanistic analysis (subtracts points if no theoretical explanation for observed phenomena).
Requires clear novel algorithmic or analytical contribution; downgrades incremental empirical studies that only repackage existing evaluation choices.
Insists on statistical rigor beyond aggregate curves: demands formal significance testing and analysis of confounders (penalises if missing).
Favors papers that provide interpretable explanations of why certain embeddings behave differently (rewards mechanistic insight).
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on whether the paper provides principled, generalizable explanations and novel theoretical contribution rather than primarily empirical benchmarking.

reviewer_b.name: Empirical Pragmatist
reviewer_b.background: Applied NLP researcher with extensive experience building and evaluating models in industry and academia, focused on robust empirical methodology and reproducible evaluations.
traits: Rewards comprehensive empirical evaluations that probe behaviour across data regimes and model families (adds points for thorough ablations and learning-curve analyses).
Gives credit for practical, easy-to-adopt evaluation protocols and benchmarks that improve real-world model selection (rewards actionable criteria like data efficiency).
Strongly values reproducibility: awards points if code, seeds, and data splits are released and experiments are deterministic/repeatable.
Penalises purely theoretical papers that lack empirical validation on realistic tasks (prefers evidence of practical impact).
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Prioritises experimental thoroughness, reproducibility, and practical usefulness of evaluation methods, viewing rigorous empirical insight as a primary contribution.
